notes.note: Nomina facere: to enter the debt formally in the account-books, the Roman equivalent of drawing up a promissory note. The agent Avianius offered to set the payment terms for the statue purchase at Cicero's convenience.

A Roman art-dealer and connoisseur of the 40s BC, on whom Cicero hoped to offload the unwanted statues; he coins 'pseudo-Damasippus' for any rival dealer. Plausibly the bankrupt collector-turned-Stoic mocked by Horace, Satires 2.3.
Statues of Bacchae (maenads, the ecstatic female devotees of Bacchus/Dionysus), a popular decorative subject. Cicero dismisses them as 'pretty little things' with no fit place in his house.

The celebrated statue-group of the Muses housed in the Porticus Metelli at Rome, built by Q. Caecilius Metellus Macedonicus (cos. 143 BC); the gold standard against which Gallus rashly compared his Bacchae.

notes.anchor: in the wrestling-school, a place after the likeness of a gymnasium
notes.note: Cicero buys statues only to furnish his palaestra (private exercise-court) in the manner of a Greek gymnasium, whose colonnades were conventionally adorned with images of the Muses, athletes, and patron deities. Bacchae and a Mars do not fit that scheme.
Cicero's self-description (pacis auctor) drives the joke: a statue of Mars, god of war, is useless to a man who styles himself peacemaker.

notes.anchor: I am glad there was no Saturn
notes.note: A pun: the temple of Saturn held the state treasury (aerarium), so Cicero quips that a Saturn statue, paired with a Mars, would have 'brought him debt' (aes alienum).

notes.anchor: I should rather there had been some Mercury
notes.note: Mercury was the god of commerce and gain, so Cicero jokes that a Mercury among the statues might have helped him strike a more favourable bargain with the dealer Avianius.
Trapezophorum: an ornamental, often marble, supporting leg or pedestal for a luxury table, a prized item of Roman interior decoration.
